⊢ Integrable (fun a => f (a i)) (Measure.pi μ)

have h_prod : Integrable (fun a : Fin d → ℝ => f (a i)) (Measure.pi μ) := by
    have h1 : Integrable (fun x : ℝ => f x) (μ i) := hf
    have h2 : (fun (a : Fin d → ℝ) => a i) = (fun (a : Fin d → ℝ) => a i) := rfl

      -- Use the fact that f is integrable on the i-th component to show that f ∘ (fun a => a i) is integrable on the product space
      apply MeasureTheory.integrable_pi_of_isolated
      <;> simp_all [Function.funext_iff]
      <;> aesop
    exact h3
  -- The result follows directly from the above
  exact h_prod

have h : Integrable (fun a ↦ f (a i)) (Measure.pi μ) := by
    classical
    have h₁ : Integrable f (μ i) := hf
    -- Use the fact that the product of measures allows us to integrate each component separately
    have h₂ : Integrable (fun a ↦ f (a i)) (Measure.pi μ) := by
      apply MeasureTheory.integrable_pi_iff.mpr
      -- For each j, we need to show that the function is integrable with respect to μ j
      intro j
      -- For each j, the function is either f if j = i, or a constant otherwise
      by_cases hj : j = i
      · -- If j = i, the function is f, which is integrable by assumption
        simp_all [hj]
        <;>
        exact hf
      · -- If j ≠ i, the function is a constant (with respect to μ j), hence integrable
        refine' integrable_const_mul _ _
        <;>
        simp_all [hj]
        <;>
        infer_instance
    exact h₂
  exact h

have h_main : Integrable (fun a ↦ f (a i)) (Measure.pi μ) := by
    -- Use the fact that the product of probability measures is a probability measure
    -- and the integrability of f with respect to μ i to deduce the integrability
    -- of the function (fun a ↦ f (a i)) with respect to the product measure.
    have h1 : Integrable (fun a : Fin d → ℝ => f (a i)) (Measure.pi μ) := by
      -- Use the fact that the product measure is the product of the measures μ i
      -- and that f is integrable with respect to μ i to deduce the integrability
      -- of the function (fun a ↦ f (a i)) with respect to the product measure.
      apply MeasureTheory.integrable_of_isCompact_of_bounded (isCompact_pi_Icc _) _
      <;> (try simp_all [IsProbabilityMeasure, MeasureTheory.MeasureSpace, MeasurableSpace])
      <;> (try norm_num)
      <;> (try
        {
          -- Show that the function is bounded
          have h2 : BddAbove (Set.range (fun (a : Fin d → ℝ) => f (a i))) := by
            -- Use the fact that f is integrable with respect to μ i to deduce that it is bounded
            have h3 : Integrable f (μ i) := hf
            have h4 : BddAbove (Set.range f) := by
              -- Use the fact that f is integrable with respect to μ i to deduce that it is bounded
              apply integrable_iff_bddAbove_ae.mp h3 |>.mono
              <;> simp [Set.subset_def]
              <;> aesop
            -- Use the fact that the range of f is bounded to deduce that the range of (fun a ↦ f (a i)) is bounded
            have h5 : BddAbove (Set.range (fun (a : Fin d → ℝ) => f (a i))) := by
              -- Use the fact that the range of f is bounded to deduce that the range of (fun a ↦ f (a i)) is bounded
              have h6 : Set.range (fun (a : Fin d → ℝ) => f (a i)) ⊆ Set.range f := by
                -- Show that the range of (fun a ↦ f (a i)) is a subset of the range of f
                intro x hx
                rcases hx with ⟨a, rfl⟩
                exact ⟨a i, by simp⟩
              -- Use the fact that the range of (fun a ↦ f (a i)) is a subset of the range of f to deduce that it is bounded
              exact BddAbove.mono h6 h4
            -- Conclude that the range of (fun a ↦ f (a i)) is bounded
            exact h5
          -- Use the fact that the range of (fun a ↦ f (a i)) is bounded to deduce that the function is bounded
          simpa using h2
        })
      <;> (try aesop)
      <;> (try
        {
          -- Show that the function is measurable
          have h2 : Measurable (fun (a : Fin d → ℝ) => f (a i)) (MeasurableSpace.pi (fun _ : Fin d => measurableSpaceℝ)) := by
            -- Use the fact that the projection is measurable and f is measurable
            apply Measurable.comp (measurable_pi_i i) hf.2.measurable
          -- Use the fact that the function is measurable to deduce that it is measurable with respect to the product measure
          exact h2
        })
    -- Conclude that the function is integrable with respect to the product measure
    exact h1
  -- Use the fact that the function is integrable with respect to the product measure to deduce the result
  exact h_main
